questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 \end{vmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: For a $2 \times 2$ matrix the determinant is $ad - bc$. Here $a=3$, $b=5$, $c=2$, $d=4$, so the value is $3 \cdot 4 - 5 \cdot 2 = 12 - 10 = 2$. The main-diagonal product comes first and the anti-diagonal product is subtracted. The trap option $22$ adds the two products as $ad + bc$ instead of subtracting.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 6 & 1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{vmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Apply $ad - bc$ with $a=6$, $b=1$, $c=-2$, $d=3$. This gives $6 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot (-2) = 18 + 2 = 20$. Subtracting a negative anti-diagonal product adds to the total. The trap option $16$ comes from computing $6 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 2 = 16$, dropping the sign on the $-2$ entry.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: What is the determinant of the diagonal matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$?
questions.explanation: The determinant of a diagonal or triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal entries. Here that product is $4 \cdot 5 \cdot 2 = 40$. The off-diagonal zeros contribute nothing to the cofactor expansion. The trap option $11$ adds the diagonal entries instead of multiplying them.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: What is the determinant of the upper triangular matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 7 & -3 \\ 0 & 3 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$?
questions.explanation: For a triangular matrix the determinant equals the product of the diagonal entries, regardless of the entries above the diagonal. That product is $2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 30$. The numbers $7$, $-3$, and $8$ above the diagonal play no role. The trap option $210$ multiplies in the entry $7$ as well, treating an off-diagonal number as if it mattered.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: A matrix is invertible if and only if which condition holds?
options: All of its entries are nonzero.
questions.explanation: A square matrix is invertible exactly when its determinant is nonzero, since the inverse formula divides by that determinant. A zero determinant signals that the rows are linearly dependent and no inverse exists. The value of the determinant need not be $1$; any nonzero number works. The trap option requiring determinant $0$ states the singular case, which is precisely when the matrix fails to be invertible.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Two square matrices $A$ and $B$ differ by a single row swap: $B$ is $A$ with two of its rows interchanged. If $\det(A) = 9$, what is $\det(B)$?
questions.explanation: Interchanging two rows of a matrix multiplies its determinant by $-1$. So $\det(B) = -\det(A) = -9$. The magnitude is unchanged; only the sign flips. The trap option $9$ assumes a row swap leaves the determinant alone, which is the rule for adding a multiple of one row to another, not for swapping.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} -4 & 7 \\ 3 & -2 \end{vmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Use $ad - bc$ with $a=-4$, $b=7$, $c=3$, $d=-2$. The main-diagonal product is $(-4)(-2) = 8$ and the anti-diagonal product is $7 \cdot 3 = 21$, giving $8 - 21 = -13$. Keep the signs of both products throughout. The trap option $29$ comes from $ad + bc = 8 + 21$, adding the two products instead of subtracting.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 6 \end{vmatrix}$ by cofactor expansion along the first row.
questions.explanation: Expanding along the first row, $\det = 1 \cdot (4 \cdot 6 - 5 \cdot 0) - 2 \cdot (0 \cdot 6 - 5 \cdot 1) + 3 \cdot (0 \cdot 0 - 4 \cdot 1)$. The minors give $1 \cdot 24 - 2 \cdot (-5) + 3 \cdot (-4) = 24 + 10 - 12 = 22$. The middle term carries the checkerboard minus sign, so $-2 \cdot (-5) = +10$. The trap option $2$ forgets that minus and uses $+2 \cdot (-5) = -10$, giving $24 - 10 - 12 = 2$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & 0 \end{vmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Expand along the first row. The first cofactor is $2 \cdot ((-1)(0) - 2 \cdot 4) = 2 \cdot (-8) = -16$, the middle term vanishes because its entry is $0$, and the third cofactor is $1 \cdot (3 \cdot 4 - (-1)(1)) = 1 \cdot 13 = 13$. Summing gives $-16 + 0 + 13 = -3$. The minor $0 - 8 = -8$ must keep its negative sign. The trap option $29$ comes from a sign slip that treats the first minor as $+8$, yielding $16 + 13$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ be a $2 \times 2$ matrix with $\det(A) = 5$. What is $\det(3A)$?
questions.explanation: Scaling an $n \times n$ matrix by $k$ multiplies the determinant by $k^n$. With $n = 2$ and $k = 3$, the factor is $3^2 = 9$, so $\det(3A) = 9 \cdot 5 = 45$. Each of the two rows is scaled by $3$, contributing one factor of $3$ apiece. The trap option $15$ uses $k \cdot \det = 3 \cdot 5$, forgetting that every row scales.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A$ be a $3 \times 3$ matrix with $\det(A) = 2$. What is $\det(2A)$?
questions.explanation: For an $n \times n$ matrix, $\det(kA) = k^n \det(A)$. Here $n = 3$ and $k = 2$, so the scaling factor is $2^3 = 8$ and $\det(2A) = 8 \cdot 2 = 16$. All three rows pick up a factor of $2$. The trap option $4$ computes $k \cdot \det = 2 \cdot 2$, applying only one factor of the scalar instead of $k^3$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ and $B$ be $3 \times 3$ matrices with $\det(A) = 4$ and $\det(B) = -3$. What is $\det(AB)$?
questions.explanation: The determinant is multiplicative: $\det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B)$. So $\det(AB) = 4 \cdot (-3) = -12$. This holds for any two square matrices of the same size, no matter the entries. The trap option $1$ adds the determinants as $4 + (-3)$, but $\det$ does not distribute over a product as a sum.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A$ be a square matrix with $\det(A) = -6$. What is $\det(A^{T})$?
questions.explanation: Transposing a matrix never changes its determinant, since $\det(A^{T}) = \det(A)$. Therefore $\det(A^{T}) = -6$, sign included. Rows become columns, but the cofactor expansion yields the same value either way. The trap option $6$ flips the sign, as if transposing behaved like a single row swap.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ be an invertible matrix with $\det(A) = 8$. What is $\det(A^{-1})$?
questions.explanation: Since $A A^{-1} = I$ and $\det(I) = 1$, the multiplicative rule gives $\det(A)\det(A^{-1}) = 1$, so $\det(A^{-1}) = \tfrac{1}{\det(A)} = \tfrac{1}{8}$. The determinant of the inverse is the reciprocal of the original. The trap option $-8$ negates the determinant instead of taking its reciprocal.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: The matrix $B$ is obtained from $A$ by adding $4$ times row $1$ to row $2$. If $\det(A) = 7$, what is $\det(B)$?
questions.explanation: Adding a multiple of one row to another row leaves the determinant completely unchanged. So $\det(B) = \det(A) = 7$. This is the row operation used in Gaussian elimination precisely because it preserves the determinant. The trap option $28$ multiplies the determinant by $4$, confusing this operation with scaling a row.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: The matrix $B$ is obtained from $A$ by multiplying every entry of row $3$ by $5$. If $\det(A) = 6$, what is $\det(B)$?
questions.explanation: Multiplying a single row by a scalar $k$ multiplies the whole determinant by that same $k$ (one factor, because only one row scales). So $\det(B) = 5 \cdot 6 = 30$. This differs from scaling the entire matrix, which would raise $5$ to the power of the matrix size. The trap option $750$ uses $5^3 \cdot 6$, applying the $k^n$ rule meant for scaling all rows at once.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Use Cramer's rule to find $x$ in the system $\begin{cases} 2x + 3y = 8 \\ x + 4y = 9 \end{cases}$.
questions.explanation: The coefficient determinant is $\begin{vmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 8 - 3 = 5$. For $x$, replace the first column with the constants: $\begin{vmatrix} 8 & 3 \\ 9 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 32 - 27 = 5$, so $x = 5/5 = 1$. The replaced column must match the variable being solved. The trap option $2$ replaces the second column instead of the first, which is the recipe for $y$, not $x$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Use Cramer's rule to find $y$ in the system $\begin{cases} 3x + y = 5 \\ 2x - y = 0 \end{cases}$.
questions.explanation: The coefficient determinant is $\begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{vmatrix} = -3 - 2 = -5$. For $y$, replace the second column with the constants: $\begin{vmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 2 & 0 \end{vmatrix} = 0 - 10 = -10$, so $y = (-10)/(-5) = 2$. The numerator and denominator determinants must be divided in that order. The trap option $1$ comes from solving $x$ by mistake, replacing the first column instead of the second.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: For which value of $k$ is the matrix $\begin{pmatrix} k & 2 \\ 8 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ singular?
questions.explanation: A matrix is singular when its determinant is zero. Here $\det = 4k - 16$, and setting $4k - 16 = 0$ gives $k = 4$. At that value the second row is twice the first, so the rows are proportional. The trap option $16$ solves $\det = 0$ but forgets to divide by the coefficient $4$, stopping at $4k = 16$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: What is the value of $\begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 & 4 \\ 6 & 2 & 8 \\ 5 & 0 & 7 \end{vmatrix}$?
questions.explanation: Row $2$ is exactly twice row $1$, since $(6, 2, 8) = 2 \cdot (3, 1, 4)$. A matrix with two proportional rows is singular, so its determinant is $0$ without any expansion needed. Proportional rows make the rows linearly dependent. The trap option $14$ would result from expanding while overlooking that the second row is a multiple of the first.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 5 & -2 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & -1 \\ 2 & 0 & 4 \end{vmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Expand along the first column: $\det = 5 \cdot (3 \cdot 4 - (-1)(0)) - 0 + 2 \cdot ((-2)(-1) - 1 \cdot 3)$. This is $5 \cdot 12 + 2 \cdot (2 - 3) = 60 + 2 \cdot (-1) = 60 - 2 = 58$. A Sarrus check confirms it: $60 + 4 + 0 - 6 - 0 - 0 = 58$. The trap option $70$ drops the minus on the $-6$ term in the Sarrus sum, adding $+6$ instead of subtracting it.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Use Cramer's rule to find $x$ in the system $\begin{cases} 4x + y = 9 \\ x + 2y = 4 \end{cases}$.
questions.explanation: The coefficient determinant is $\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} = 8 - 1 = 7$. For $x$, replace the first column with the constants: $\begin{vmatrix} 9 & 1 \\ 4 & 2 \end{vmatrix} = 18 - 4 = 14$, so $x = 14/7 = 2$. Cramer's rule puts the replaced determinant on top and the coefficient determinant on the bottom. The trap option $\tfrac{1}{2}$ swaps numerator and denominator, computing $7/14$ instead of $14/7$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A$ and $B$ be $2 \times 2$ matrices with $\det(A) = 3$ and $\det(B) = 6$. What is $\det(A^{-1} B)$?
questions.explanation: Using $\det(A^{-1}) = 1/\det(A)$ and the product rule, $\det(A^{-1} B) = \det(A^{-1})\det(B) = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot 6 = 2$. The inverse contributes the reciprocal of $\det(A)$, which then multiplies $\det(B)$. The trap option $18$ multiplies $\det(A)$ by $\det(B)$ directly, ignoring that the inverse inverts the determinant.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ and $B$ be $2 \times 2$ matrices with $\det(A) = 4$ and $\det(B) = 9$. It is true that $\det(A + B) = 36$. Is this statement correct, and why?
options: Incorrect, because $\det$ is not additive or multiplicative over a sum, so $\det(A + B)$ is not determined by $\det(A)$ and $\det(B)$ alone.
Correct, because the determinant of a sum is the product of the determinants.
questions.explanation: There is no rule that turns $\det(A + B)$ into any fixed combination of $\det(A)$ and $\det(B)$; the determinant is multiplicative over products but not over sums. So the value $36$ cannot be justified, and in general $\det(A + B)$ depends on the actual entries, not just the two determinants. The claim is therefore incorrect. The trap is assuming $\det(A + B) = \det(A) + \det(B)$ or $\det(A)\det(B)$, neither of which holds for sums.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: For which value of $k$ does the matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & k & 0 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ have determinant $0$?
questions.explanation: Expanding along the first row, $\det = 1 \cdot (3 \cdot 2 - 1 \cdot 1) - k \cdot (2 \cdot 2 - 1 \cdot 0) + 0 = (6 - 1) - k(4) = 5 - 4k$. Setting $5 - 4k = 0$ gives $k = \tfrac{5}{4}$. The middle cofactor carries the checkerboard minus sign, which is essential here. The trap option $\tfrac{5}{2}$ drops the factor of $4$ from the second cofactor, solving $5 - 2k = 0$ instead.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Use Cramer's rule to find $z$ in the system $\begin{cases} x + y + z = 6 \\ 2y + z = 4 \\ x + z = 3 \end{cases}$.
questions.explanation: The coefficient matrix is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ with determinant $1(2 - 0) - 1(0 - 1) + 1(0 - 2) = 2 + 1 - 2 = 1$. For $z$, replace the third column with the constants $(6, 4, 3)$: $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 1 & 6 \\ 0 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \end{vmatrix} = 1(2 \cdot 3 - 4 \cdot 0) - 1(0 \cdot 3 - 4 \cdot 1) + 6(0 \cdot 0 - 2 \cdot 1) = 6 + 4 - 12 = -2$, so $z = -2/1 = -2$. The replaced column must be the one matching $z$, namely the third. The trap option $5$ replaces the first column instead, which is the recipe for $x$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -1 \\ -2 & 1 & 4 \end{vmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: By Sarrus, the three forward-diagonal products are $(1)(0)(4) + (-1)(-1)(-2) + (2)(3)(1) = 0 - 2 + 6 = 4$, and the three backward-diagonal products are $(2)(0)(-2) + (1)(-1)(1) + (-1)(3)(4) = 0 - 1 - 12 = -13$. The determinant is forward minus backward: $4 - (-13) = 17$. The backward products must be subtracted as a block, which flips their signs. The trap option $-7$ comes from mishandling those signs, subtracting where the rule adds.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ be a $3 \times 3$ matrix with $\det(A) = -2$. What is $\det(A^2)$?
questions.explanation: Since $A^2 = A \cdot A$, the product rule gives $\det(A^2) = \det(A)\det(A) = (-2)(-2) = 4$. Squaring the matrix squares the determinant, and squaring a negative number gives a positive result. The trap option $-4$ keeps a negative sign, as if the determinant of a square could stay negative.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 7 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{vmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Apply $ad - bc$ with $a=7$, $b=-3$, $c=-5$, $d=2$. The main-diagonal product is $7 \cdot 2 = 14$ and the anti-diagonal product is $(-3)(-5) = 15$, so $\det = 14 - 15 = -1$. Both negative entries multiply to a positive $15$ before it is subtracted. The trap option $29$ comes from $14 - (-15)$, mishandling the sign of the anti-diagonal product.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ be a $4 \times 4$ matrix with $\det(A) = 3$. What is $\det(-A)$?
questions.explanation: Negating a matrix is scaling by $k = -1$, so $\det(-A) = (-1)^n \det(A)$ for an $n \times n$ matrix. With $n = 4$, $(-1)^4 = 1$, hence $\det(-A) = 1 \cdot 3 = 3$. The even size makes the sign positive. The trap option $-3$ assumes the sign always flips, ignoring that the exponent $n$ is even here.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 5 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{vmatrix}$ and decide whether the matrix is invertible.
questions.solution: The $2 \times 2$ rule computes this determinant as {{1}}. Carrying that out gives {{2}}. A square matrix is invertible exactly when its determinant is {{3}}, so this matrix {{4}}.

options: cannot be classified from the determinant alone
is invertible only if it is also symmetric

questions.explanation: The $2 \times 2$ determinant is $ad - bc$: the main-diagonal product minus the anti-diagonal product. Here that is $5 \cdot 4 - 3 \cdot 2 = 20 - 6 = 14$. Adding the two products as $5 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 2 = 26$ is the classic sign trap, while $5 \cdot 3 - 2 \cdot 4$ pairs the entries across rows and $5 \cdot 2 - 3 \cdot 4$ pairs them down columns, neither of which is the diagonal rule. Invertibility asks only that the determinant be nonzero; it does not need to be positive or equal to $1$. Since $14 \neq 0$, the matrix is invertible, and symmetry has nothing to do with it.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Compute the determinant of the diagonal matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$ and decide whether the matrix is invertible.
questions.solution: A diagonal matrix is a special case of a triangular matrix, so its determinant equals {{1}}. Here that gives {{2}}. From this value we conclude that the matrix {{3}}.

options: the product of the diagonal entries
the sum of the diagonal entries
zero, because most of the entries are zero
the product of the absolute values of the diagonal entries
gaps.answer: the product of the diagonal entries

options: is invertible, because the determinant is nonzero
is not invertible, because the determinant is negative
is not invertible, because the matrix contains zero entries
cannot be classified without row reducing
gaps.answer: is invertible, because the determinant is nonzero

questions.explanation: For a triangular (hence diagonal) matrix the determinant is the product of the diagonal entries: $3 \cdot (-2) \cdot 5 = -30$. The many zero entries do not force a zero determinant; they merely kill every other term of the cofactor expansion. Summing the diagonal gives the trace, $6$, which is a different invariant, and taking absolute values first would give $30$ and destroy the sign, which the determinant carries. Invertibility only requires a nonzero determinant, so $-30$ qualifies: a negative determinant is perfectly invertible, and no row reduction is needed once the determinant is known.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the determinant $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 3 & -1 & 4 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$ by expansion along the first row.
questions.solution: Each term of the expansion is an entry times its cofactor. The entry $1$ in position $(1,1)$ contributes $1 \cdot ((-1)(1) - (4)(0)) = -1$. For the entry $2$ in position $(1,2)$, the minor is the determinant of {{1}}, and once the checkerboard sign is attached the full second term equals {{2}}. The entry $0$ wipes out the third term, so the determinant is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The minor of the entry in row $1$, column $2$ deletes that row and that column, leaving $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ with determinant $3 - 8 = -5$; the other candidates delete the wrong row or column. The checkerboard sign at position $(1,2)$ is negative, so the second term is $-2 \cdot (-5) = 10$. Forgetting that sign gives $-10$, reusing the $(1,1)$ minor gives $2$, and a sign slip inside the minor ($3 + 8 = 11$) leads to $-22$. Summing the terms, $-1 + 10 + 0 = 9$; the zero entry spares us the third cofactor entirely.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ be a $3 \times 3$ matrix with $\det(A) = 4$. What is $\det(2A)$?
questions.solution: The scalar $2$ multiplies every entry of $A$. In determinant terms, the correct rule multiplies $\det(A)$ by {{1}}, because {{2}}. Applying the rule to this matrix gives $\det(2A) =$ {{3}}.

options: each of the $n$ rows picks up its own factor of $k$
the scalar acts once on the matrix as a whole
each row and each column is scaled, giving $2n$ factors of $k$
determinants add across rows, so the factors of $k$ add up
gaps.answer: each of the $n$ rows picks up its own factor of $k$

questions.explanation: Scaling $A$ by $k$ scales all $n$ rows, and the determinant is linear in each row separately, so each row contributes one factor of $k$: $\det(kA) = k^n \det(A)$. Treating the scalar as acting once gives only $k$, letting the factors add gives $nk$, and rows and columns are not scaled independently, so $2n$ factors would double count. Here $n = 3$ and $k = 2$, so the factor is $2^3 = 8$ and $\det(2A) = 8 \cdot 4 = 32$. The $2 \times 2$ habit of squaring the scalar would give $4 \cdot 4 = 16$, which is wrong for a $3 \times 3$ matrix.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A$ and $B$ be $3 \times 3$ matrices with $\det(A) = 5$ and $\det(B) = -2$. What is $\det(AB)$?
questions.solution: The determinant interacts with a matrix product through the rule {{1}}. Applying it here gives $\det(AB) =$ {{2}}. And although $BA$ need not equal $AB$ as a matrix, $\det(BA)$ is {{3}}.

options: $\det(AB)$ is not determined by $\det(A)$ and $\det(B)$ alone
the opposite sign, since reversing the factors acts like a row swap
not determined, since $BA \neq AB$ in general

questions.explanation: The determinant is multiplicative: $\det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B) = 5 \cdot (-2) = -10$. It is sums that break down: $\det(A + B)$ really is undetermined by the two determinants alone, but a product is fully determined, and no size factor $n$ enters the rule. For the reversed product, $\det(BA) = \det(B)\det(A)$, and multiplication of numbers commutes, so $\det(BA) = -10$ as well even though $BA$ and $AB$ can differ as matrices. Reversing the factors is not a row swap, so no sign flips, and nothing here is inverted, so no reciprocal appears.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: The matrix $B$ is obtained from $A$ by interchanging two of its rows, and $C$ is then obtained from $B$ by interchanging two rows again. If $\det(A) = 7$, find $\det(B)$ and $\det(C)$.
questions.solution: A single row interchange {{1}}. Therefore $\det(B) =$ {{2}}. The second interchange applies the same rule once more, so $\det(C) =$ {{3}}.

options: rescales the determinant by an amount that depends on the swapped rows

questions.explanation: Interchanging two rows multiplies the determinant by $-1$: the magnitude survives and only the sign flips, so $\det(B) = -7$. It is the other elementary operation, adding a multiple of one row to another, that leaves the determinant unchanged, and no swap can turn a nonzero determinant into zero. The effect also never depends on which rows are swapped. The second interchange multiplies by $-1$ again, and $(-1)(-1) = 1$, so $\det(C) = 7$: two swaps restore the original sign. Thinking the sign stays flipped gives $-7$, and imagining the effects add gives $14$; both misread how the two factors of $-1$ combine.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: For which value of $k$ is the matrix $\begin{pmatrix} k & 5 \\ 3 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ singular?
questions.solution: Singular means the matrix fails to be invertible, which happens exactly when {{1}}. In terms of $k$, the determinant of this matrix is {{2}}. Imposing the singularity condition and solving gives $k =$ {{3}}. At that value of $k$ the failure is visible directly in the matrix, because {{4}}.

options: it has at least one zero entry
one of the rows becomes entirely zero
the two diagonal entries become equal

questions.explanation: A matrix is singular exactly when its determinant is zero; the sign or size of a nonzero determinant is irrelevant, and having a zero entry proves nothing. By $ad - bc$ the determinant is $k \cdot 6 - 5 \cdot 3 = 6k - 15$; adding the products gives $6k + 15$, while $5k - 18$ and $3k - 30$ pair the entries across the wrong diagonals. Solving $6k - 15 = 0$ gives $k = 2.5$. At that value the second row $(3, 6)$ is $1.2$ times the first row $(2.5, 5)$, so the rows are proportional and hence linearly dependent, which is what singularity looks like in a $2 \times 2$ matrix. No row becomes zero, and symmetry or equal diagonal entries never enter the story.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Use Cramer's rule to solve $\begin{cases} 2x + y = 5 \\ 3x + 4y = 10 \end{cases}$ for $x$ and $y$.
questions.solution: Cramer's rule writes each unknown as a ratio of two determinants. The denominator, the same for both unknowns, is {{1}}. The numerator for $x$ is {{2}}. Evaluating the two determinants and dividing gives $x =$ {{3}}. Building the numerator for $y$ the same way and dividing gives $y =$ {{4}}.

options: the determinant of the coefficient matrix
the determinant with the constants substituted into the column of the unknown
the product of the diagonal entries of the coefficient matrix
the trace of the coefficient matrix
gaps.answer: the determinant of the coefficient matrix

questions.explanation: The denominator is always the coefficient determinant $D = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 8 - 3 = 5$; it does not change between unknowns, it is not the trace, and the diagonal-product shortcut is only valid for triangular matrices. The numerator for $x$ replaces the $x$ column, the first one, with the constants: $\begin{vmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 10 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 20 - 10 = 10$, so $x = 10/5 = 2$. Replacing the second column instead builds the numerator for $y$, and pushing the constants into a row is not a legal substitution at all. For $y$, $\begin{vmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 10 \end{vmatrix} = 20 - 15 = 5$, so $y = 5/5 = 1$. Check: $2(2) + 1 = 5$ and $3(2) + 4(1) = 10$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A$ be an invertible matrix with $\det(A) = -4$. Find $\det(A^{T})$ and $\det(A^{-1})$.
questions.solution: For the transpose, the governing identity is $\det(A^{T}) =$ {{1}}. For the inverse, $\det(A^{-1})$ equals {{2}}, so evaluating it for this matrix gives $\det(A^{-1}) =$ {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Transposing swaps rows with columns, but the cofactor expansion produces the same value either way, so $\det(A^{T}) = \det(A) = -4$: a transpose is not a row swap and does not flip the sign, and nothing gets squared or inverted. For the inverse, take determinants of both sides of $A A^{-1} = I$: since $\det(I) = 1$, we get $\det(A)\det(A^{-1}) = 1$, so $\det(A^{-1})$ is the reciprocal $1/(-4) = -0.25$. The reciprocal keeps the sign of the original; dropping the sign to get $0.25$ is the trap of using $|\det(A)|$, and $\det(A^{-1})$ could only equal $\det(A)$ if the determinant were $\pm 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra
